children.text: The Panama Canal operates within fixed borders, yet its hydrological system is continuously reconfigured through technical and legal adjustments to its watershed.

In 2024, the Panama Canal Authority (ACP) announced an adjustment to the Canal watershed boundaries, expanding the spatial framework through which water is managed, monitored and allocated. This redefinition actively reorganized territory by incorporating new rural basins into Canal's operational domain.

The expansion of watershed limits reproduces territorial effects in advance of material infrastructure. With new boundaries the Canal deploys new technical studies, environmental assessments, and planning processes that render projects such as the proposed Río Indio reservoir adminsitratively viable and strategically necessary.

This is not the first time the Canal watershed has been redefined. When the administration of the waterway was transfered from US to Panamanian control in 1999, a campesino protest movement emerged in response to a proposal that would have displaced a large number of rural communities from their lands in order to create new lakes for the Panama Canal. Law 44 effectively expanded the Canal watershed by adding approximately 213,000 hectares to the existing 312,00 hectares Eastern Watershed.

children.caption: The new limits of the Panama Canal watershed. Source: La Prensa, Panama.

title: Watershed Expansion of the Panama Canal
